title: How to Choose the Best Moving Company in Orlando (Without Getting Burned by Hidden Fees)
excerpt: Best moving companies Orlando locals trust share one thing: no hidden fees. Learn what to look for before you book — and what red flags to avoid.
metaDescription: Best moving companies Orlando locals trust share one thing: no hidden fees. Learn what to look for before you book — and what red flags to avoid.
author: Liberty Moves Orlando
heroImageAlt: How to Choose the Best Moving Company in Orlando (Without Getting Burned by Hidden Fees)
contentHtml:
You've found a new place. You've set a date. And now you're staring at a list of "best moving companies in Orlando" on Google, wondering which one won't turn a $400 estimate into a $900 invoice by the time the last box hits the floor.

It happens more than it should. And in Florida — where moving scams are common enough that the state passed a dedicated anti-fraud law in 2024 — it's worth knowing exactly what to look for before you hand anyone your house keys and your couch.

This guide breaks down what separates a trustworthy Orlando mover from one that'll cost you.

Start With the License, Not the Reviews

Reviews matter. But a five-star rating doesn't tell you whether a company is legally allowed to move your belongings. In Florida, that's a separate question — and the answer is public.

Every intrastate mover (meaning any company moving you within Florida) must be registered with the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services (FDACS) and hold an IM number. Interstate movers — those taking you across state lines — must also carry a USDOT number, which you can verify for free through the FMCSA's website.

If a company can't produce both numbers when you ask, stop the conversation there.

Liberty Moves Orlando carries USDOT 3455436 and FLDACS IM3347 — both verifiable in 30 seconds online. Any legitimate Orlando moving company should be able to say the same.

The Estimate Is Where Hidden Fees Are Born

The most common way Orlando movers burn customers isn't a bait-and-switch on the day of the move — it's a vague estimate on the front end.

Here's what to watch for:

Non-binding verbal quotes. If your estimate wasn't put in writing and signed by both parties, it's not an estimate — it's a conversation. Always get a written, itemized quote.

Hourly rates without caps or assumptions. "Starting at $X/hour" sounds affordable until the crew takes three hours longer than expected and you have no recourse. Ask what's included in the rate, how they handle delays, and whether there's a minimum.

"We'll quote the rest on-site" language. Some companies provide a low base rate, then add fuel surcharges, stair fees, long-carry fees, elevator fees, and packing material charges once they arrive and your furniture is already on the truck. At that point, you have very little leverage.

What to ask instead:

  • Is this estimate binding?
  • What additional charges could apply to my specific move?
  • Do you charge for stairs, long carries, or elevator waits?
  • Is there a fuel surcharge, and is it included in this quote?

A company offering transparent flat-rate pricing will answer all of these without hesitation. If a mover gets cagey, that hesitation is the answer.

Local vs. National: What "Best Long Distance Moving Company" Really Means for Orlando Movers

If you're searching for the best long distance moving companies from Orlando, the results will mostly surface national brands — large carriers like United Van Lines, Allied, or North American. These aren't bad options, but they come with a tradeoff most people don't expect: the crew that shows up at your door may be a subcontractor, not an employee of the company you hired.

That matters for a few reasons:

  • You don't always know who's handling your stuff
  • Accountability is diluted across multiple parties
  • Claims for damage can become a months-long back-and-forth between carrier and broker

A local long distance moving company that self-performs the move — meaning the same team that loads you also drives and delivers — gives you a cleaner chain of custody and a single point of contact if anything goes wrong.

For moves within Florida or to neighboring states, a locally-based Orlando mover with long-distance authority is often the sharper call.

What "Insured" Actually Means (Read This Before You Assume You're Covered)

Every legitimate moving company carries insurance. But what most customers don't realize is that released value protection — the default coverage included in every move — only pays out 60 cents per pound per item. Your 50-lb flat screen TV: $30. Your antique dresser: maybe $15.

That's not insurance. That's a liability floor.

If you have high-value items, ask specifically about full value protection — a separate, optional coverage that replaces or repairs items at current market value. This usually costs more, but it's the only version of moving insurance that actually functions like insurance.

Two things to confirm before booking any mover:

1. Their liability coverage type and limits

2. Whether third-party moving insurance is available through their quote process

Red Flags to Walk Away From

You don't need to be a moving industry expert to spot a problem. Most of the bad actors leave obvious signals:

  • No physical address or a P.O. Box as their business location
  • No license numbers available on the website or when asked directly
  • A quote dramatically lower than every other company you've spoken with
  • Demands a large cash deposit before the move date
  • Generic company name with no reviews older than 6 months (rebrand after negative review history is a common pattern in the scam-mover playbook)
  • No written contract offered before moving day

Florida's 2024 moving law (Chapter 507 updates) added new consumer protections around estimates, deposits, and holdbacks — but those protections only apply if you've hired a licensed carrier. The simplest protection is starting with a licensed, insured mover.

The Bottom Line

The best moving company in Orlando isn't always the cheapest one on the list or the most-advertised name. It's the one that shows you its license when you ask, puts the price in writing before the truck pulls up, and has a crew that actually knows how to move a house.

When you're comparing moving companies near me in Central Florida, Liberty Moves Orlando is licensed, insured, family-owned, and fully transparent on pricing. No surprise fees. No subcontractors. No runaround.

Get a free written quote at libertymovesorlando.com or call (407) 641-2887 — and ask us anything before you book.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: How do I verify a moving company's license in Florida? \
A: Search the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services (FDACS) mover directory at fdacs.gov using the company name or IM number. For interstate movers, verify the USDOT number at safer.fmcsa.dot.gov. Both lookups are free and take under a minute.

Q: What's the difference between a binding and non-binding moving estimate? \
A: A binding estimate is a written, signed agreement that locks in the total price regardless of how long the move takes. A non-binding estimate is an approximation — the final bill can be higher. Always request a binding written estimate.

Q: Why are some Orlando moving quotes so much lower than others? \
A: Low quotes often exclude fees that appear on the final invoice: stair charges, elevator waits, fuel surcharges, and packing material costs. They may also reflect a broker quoting the job, not the company that will actually do the work. Ask exactly what's included — and get it in writing.

Q: What should I look for in a long distance moving company from Orlando? \
A: For interstate moves, confirm the company holds a valid USDOT number, verify their FMCSA safety record, ask whether they self-perform the move or broker it to subcontractors, and confirm full-value coverage is available. For moves to neighboring states (Georgia, North Carolina, Tennessee), a locally-based Orlando mover with long-distance authority is often the most accountable choice.

Q: Is Liberty Moves Orlando licensed for both local and long distance moves? \
A: Yes. Liberty Moves Orlando holds FLDACS IM3347 for intrastate moves and USDOT 3455436 for interstate moves. Both are verifiable through the official government databases linked above.
